Predictive Value: The rule predicts that the marginal cost of going from 70% to 90% certainty is higher than the marginal cost of making a wrong decision and course-correcting. This holds in fast-moving competitive environments where the window of opportunity closes before perfect information is available. It also predicts that companies with a culture of demanding certainty before action will consistently lose to companies that decide quickly and iterate. Amazon Prime was designed, built, and launched in under four months. That pace requires decision-making at 70%.

Explanatory Gap: The rule doesn't explain when the 70% threshold is appropriate versus when waiting for more information is genuinely the better call. It is not applicable to irreversible, catastrophic decisions (Bezos separately distinguishes "one-way door" decisions from "two-way door" decisions). The rule has no mechanism — it simply describes what the best operators do. A good explanation would model the tradeoff between decision speed and decision quality under different conditions.

Why It's Unintuitive: Human psychology strongly favors certainty before commitment. Loss aversion and accountability pressure make most managers want more data before acting. Bezos is arguing that this instinct, while natural, is systematically wrong in fast-moving environments. The mental model is: being wrong is recoverable; being slow is not. See also Failure and Invention are Inseparable Twins.

2023-09-21 Num 321 Working with Jeff Bezos

Marc Rich

Speed as Competitive Advantage

In commodity trading, the window between a market signal and the closing of a profitable trade can be measured in hours. Marc Rich and Company's early competitive advantage was not primarily informational — Phillip Brothers knew most of what Rich knew. It was executional. Rich's company moved faster, decided faster, and got on planes faster than anyone else.

The founding philosophy of Marc Rich and Company, as Senra describes it, was "more aggressive, faster, and more oriented toward long-term contracts" than Phillip Brothers. The operational principle was direct: "Ecuador is selling oil? Fly over there immediately and don't just buy the oil that is up for sale — get the client to agree on a long-term contract." When Rich and Pinky Green were in Houston for an oil deal and learned en route that Turkey urgently needed oil, Green didn't finish the Houston meeting — he interrupted it, flew to Istanbul, and closed a profitable trade the same trip. "We were faster than everyone else," Rich said.

Speed, in the context of commodity trading, is a force multiplier. It converts an informational edge into a positional advantage before the market corrects. It converts a first meeting into a long-term contract before a competitor lands. It converts a supply gap into a captured customer. The companies Rich competed against were operating on Phillip Brothers' philosophy: "it is better to sleep well than eat well" — prioritize avoiding tail risk over capturing upside. Rich prioritized capture.

Predictive Value: The rule predicts that in any market where goods flow through discrete, time-sensitive channels — commodities, real estate, distressed assets — execution speed will disproportionately reward firms willing to invest in the organizational culture and decision-making processes that enable it. Rich's first-year results ($1 billion in turnover, $28 million in net profit) confirm the prediction.

Explanatory Gap: The rule does not explain when speed becomes recklessness — when the pressure to move first overrides the due diligence that prevents catastrophic mistakes. The zinc trade, approved in haste under stress, is the cautionary counterexample from within the same biography.

2019-03-10 Num 63 The King of Oil - The Secret Lives of Marc Rich

Henry Kaiser

Speed as Competitive Advantage

Speed is not merely a personality trait — it is a structural advantage. Moving faster than competitors creates optionality (you lock up opportunities before others can respond), reduces negotiation costs (fast movers face less counter-bidding), and signals capability (partners and clients interpret speed as competence).

Kaiser demonstrated this at every scale: he memorized every price in a hardware store in weeks while long-tenured clerks had never bothered; he closed on the Kaiser-Frazer automobile venture in eight days (filing paperwork, raising capital, securing facilities) while his partner was accustomed to years of deliberation; once he decided he wanted to work with Howard Hughes, he "bounced into Hughes' suite and laid out a complete program" in one meeting.

The author characterizes it directly: "Once he made up his mind, he moved quickly and forcefully. Throughout his life, Kaiser dominated men who usually dominated others."

Why It's Unintuitive

The natural assumption is that careful deliberation produces better decisions. In many stable environments, this is true. Kaiser operated in environments where the opportunity closed before the deliberation ended. In those contexts, speed itself becomes part of the decision quality.

Broad Reach

In venture capital, the best investments go to the investor who calls back first. In construction bidding, the contractor who submits first often sets the reference point. In wartime production, the supplier who can promise delivery dates gets the contract. The pattern: in time-sensitive, high-stakes domains, execution speed is often more valuable than execution precision.

2019-04-07 Num 66 Henry Kaiser - Builder in the Modern American West

Liberty Ships — 1,490 Ships at Unprecedented Speed

Between 1940 and 1945, Kaiser's seven West Coast shipyards produced 1,490 Liberty ships — more than any comparable program — at a total cost of just over $4 billion in 1940s dollars. One Liberty ship was eventually launched in four days, a production record that stood as a symbol of American industrial capacity during the war.

The numbers are stark: U-boats were sinking British vessels three times faster than they could be replaced when Kaiser entered the industry. By the program's end, the equation had reversed.

Kaiser entered shipbuilding in 1940 having never seen a ship launched. He told Senator Harry Truman's committee this under oath in 1942 — by which point his yards were already the most productive in the world.

Two innovations drove the speed:

1. Welding replaced riveting. Riveting required months of skilled training. Welding could be taught to unskilled workers — including women entering the workforce for the first time as their husbands were drafted — in days. This massively expanded the available labor pool.

2. Prefabrication replaced sequential assembly. The conventional method sent hundreds of workers swarming into cramped quarters to work sequentially after a keel was laid. Kaiser's yards prefabricated large sections elsewhere, then used heavy cranes (mastered at the dam sites) to join them at the hull. Parallel work streams eliminated the central bottleneck.

Both innovations were transferred from other industries Kaiser already understood: the assembly-line coordination from road and dam building; the welding from construction; the heavy crane operation from Grand Coulee. He didn't invent new techniques — he applied existing ones across domain boundaries.

2019-04-07 Num 66 Henry Kaiser - Builder in the Modern American West

David Ogilvy

Poor, Unknown, and in a Hurry — Urgency as Competitive Weapon

Having nothing to lose is a structural advantage. When you are poor, unknown, and in a hurry, you make moves that the comfortable, established, and deliberate never will.

Ogilvy captures this in one of the great sentences of the book. Explaining why he broke the conventions of dignified professional behavior — mass direct-mail campaigns, cold visits, unsolicited speeches to potential clients — he writes: "If I had behaved in a more professional way, it would have taken me twenty years to arrive. I had neither the time nor the money to wait. I was poor, unknown, and in a hurry."

Why It's a Mental Model:

It contradicts the instinct to wait until you're ready, until you have enough resources, until conditions are favorable. The mental model says scarcity of resources accelerates decisiveness. With no cushion to fall back on, you take swings that the well-funded player would consider too risky. And sometimes that is the only way through.

Why It's Also a Rule of Thumb:

The predictive pattern is consistent across the Founders canon: early-stage companies move faster, take more creative risks, and outmaneuver established players precisely because they have to. The Founders Podcast episode makes this explicit — the established agencies eventually become extinct volcanoes (see The Extinct Volcano — Agency Lifecycle Decay). The urgency of being new is exactly what the old agencies lost.

Predictive Value:

  • Ogilvy's first clients, including Shell and the five blue-chip targets he wrote on day one, came from aggressive early solicitation that would have seemed undignified for an established firm
  • His direct-mail campaigns reached "the most august of advertisers" because he had no alternative
  • Senra generalizes: resource constraints force you to outsmart rather than outspend, which tends to produce creative solutions that persist long after the original constraint is gone

Why It Persists:

The mental model persists because comfort is its enemy. Most successful founders eventually become comfortable. The urgency that made them formidable fades. See The Extinct Volcano — Agency Lifecycle Decay for what happens next.

2019-09-15 Num 89 David Ogilvy (Confessions of an Advertising Man)

Winston Churchill

Relentless Action Bias — Speed Brevity and Working from Every Surface

Churchill's operating style had three constants: speed, brevity, and unrelenting focus on what was most important. He issued commands in brief memos — one page or less, always. His directive on the sticky bomb, a military technology under debate: "Make one million." Two words. When a minister sent him a long report, his response was often a single sentence. He had a favorite maxim for this: "It is slothful not to compress your thoughts."

He described his leadership philosophy as "get action." Anything not of immediate importance to the war effort was of no value to him. This created an atmosphere at 10 Downing Street that was qualitatively different from his predecessor's: civil servants were seen running in corridors they had previously walked. The workload increased "to unimagined levels."

He also never let the concept of "office" constrain him. The bathtub was an office. The bed was an office. A train was an office. A rooftop during a bombing was an office. He worked from the moment he woke until he slept, using a nap as a recharge mechanism rather than allowing any portion of the day to go unused.

This trait matters because it gave Churchill a productivity advantage that compounded. While Hitler's generals were managing the politics of their Führer's delusions and Goring was distracted by stolen art, Churchill was producing outputs — speeches, memos, directives, strategic decisions — at a rate that no one else in the war matched.

See Single Well-Defined Objective for the strategic complement to this operational intensity.

2021-08-07 Num 196 Winston Churchill (Leadership during WW2)

36

Luck, Timing, and the Right Moment

The role of fortune in founder success — not as randomness, but as the alignment between a specific person's capabilities and a specific historical moment. As Napoleon's biographer wrote: "The man meets his hour." Preparation creates the conditions for luck to land, but without the right moment, even the best-prepared founder builds something smaller.

  • Frederick Smith (FedEx)
  • Napoleon
  • Chuck Yeager
  • Bob Noyce (Intel)
  • Charlie Munger
  • Christian Dior
  • Oprah Winfrey
  • Richard Garriott
  • TR and JP Morgan
  • John D. Rockefeller

Frederick Smith (FedEx)

Timing as the Hidden Variable of Success

An otherwise sound idea, attempted too early, fails because the enabling conditions don't yet exist. Attempted too late, it fails because competitors have already claimed the market. Timing is not luck — it is a distinct variable that can be analyzed, and it explains outcomes that talent and effort alone cannot.

Fred Smith stated this directly: "The idea that is Federal Express absolutely positively would not have worked five years before we did it. And five years later, the market would have been so clear that somebody would have served it in one way or another." He drew the same lesson from Dwight Eisenhower — a capable but not exceptional officer who became the right person, in the right place, at the right time, and built on that position to reach the presidency.

Smith's version of the insight had a democratic implication: there are, at any moment, "a thousand Dwight Eisenhowers" and "thousands of Fred Smiths" — people with the skills and disposition to build transformative institutions, who will succeed only if they find their moment. Sam Walton made the same point in his memoir, which Senra had covered the prior week.

Why It's a Good Explanation

The explanation is constrained by specificity: it predicts not just that timing matters, but that there is a narrow window — too early and the infrastructure, regulation, or market doesn't support the idea; too late and the window closes. This is falsifiable and historically well-attested.

Hard-to-Vary Details

The window analogy is load-bearing. If timing were merely a synonym for luck, there would be no way to analyze or exploit it. Smith's formulation makes it analyzable: look for conditions that are just now becoming enabling, before the opportunity becomes obvious to all.

Reach

The same logic explains why biotech startups cluster around specific platform shifts (CRISPR, mRNA), why internet companies succeeded in the late 1990s rather than the late 1980s, and why certain business models are viable in one decade and not another.

2020-10-29 Num 151 Frederick Smith (FedEx)

Napoleon

The Man Meets His Hour — Talent Must Match Historical Moment

There is a sentence in Bell's biography that Senra essentially stops the episode to double-underline: "History is not a matter of impersonal forces and nothing ensured that an individual would come along to exploit the changes as fully and spectacularly as Napoleon. Many are the historical opportunities that have been lost for lack of talent or vision. In Napoleon's case, the man met his hour."

This is one of the most important structural insights in all of Senra's Napoleon episodes. The French Revolution created the opening. But openings do not fill themselves. For every Napoleon who rides the chaos-ladder all the way to the top, there are dozens of historically forgotten men who stood at the same ladder and lacked the talent, the hunger, the specific genius, or simply the ruthlessness to climb.

The formula has three variables: 1. Structural disruption — the hierarchy must be destabilized (the Revolution, the internet, a market shift) 2. Specific talent — skills that are newly valuable in the disrupted landscape 3. The man (or woman) who actually shows up — someone willing to operate without historical precedent

Remove any one element and the story ends differently. The Revolution without Napoleon produces a decade of chaos and then a restoration. Napoleon without the Revolution produces an exceptional general who never rises above Marshal.

Why It's Good: The explanation is hard to vary because it requires all three variables to be present. A "great man" theory alone fails — it cannot explain why equally talented men of the same era did not rise as far. A pure structuralist argument fails — it cannot explain why most people who lived through the Revolution did not become Napoleon.

Reach: The same framework explains the first wave of internet billionaires. The structural disruption was the web. The talent required was rare but definable. And in each case — Bezos, Jobs, Gates — the man had to actually show up and be willing to operate in conditions of radical uncertainty.

Related: The French Revolution as Chaos-Ladder, td-pattern — Chaos as Ladder — Disruption Creates Unprecedented Social Mobility

2023-03-13 Num 294 The Biography of Napoleon

Chuck Yeager

Born at the Right Time — Luck and Timing

The scope of a life's work is shaped not only by talent and effort but by when in history you arrive. Extraordinary individuals require extraordinary epochs.

Yeager said it directly: "I don't deny that I was damn good. But what really strikes me looking back over all those years is how lucky I was." Born in 1923, he came of age just as aviation was entering the modern era. His early 20s coincided with the golden age of aviation research — the leap from prop engines to jets, from jets to rockets, from rockets to space. He stood at every historical junction, not as a spectator but as the person pushing the boundary forward.

The analogy he offers is clean: "To make his mark on history, Christopher Columbus had to be born at a time when the world was believed to be flat. To make mine, people had to still think that the sound barrier was a brick wall in the sky."

Why It's a Good Explanation: The explanation is constrained: it doesn't say luck is everything. It says that talent and effort are necessary but not sufficient. The functional detail is the intersection — exceptional ability plus an open historical frontier. The explanation correctly predicts that the same Yeager born in 1963 would never have broken the sound barrier (someone else would have, in a different jet, under different bureaucratic conditions). Born in 1883, he might have been an excellent World War I ace, but the ceiling on achievement would have been different.

Hard-to-Vary Details: The mechanism is opportunity cost of timing. An aviator born before jets existed cannot demonstrate jet mastery. An aviator born after jets are routinized cannot demonstrate jet mastery as a historic first. The window of maximum achievement is narrow. Identifying that window is partly luck, but recognizing it and having the skills to exploit it when you're in it is not.

Reach:
Senra poses this to the listener as an actionable question: "What is today's equivalent?" What frontiers exist right now that could only be crossed by someone alive right now? This question reframes biography from nostalgia into opportunity-mapping.

2021-01-11 Num 162 Chuck Yeager

Bob Noyce (Intel)

The Fortuitous Chain of Events

Tom Wolfe pauses in the middle of his essay to stage a series of counterfactuals. What if Grant Gale had not gone to school with John Bardeen? What if Oliver Buckley had not been a Grinnell alumnus? What if Gale hadn't bothered to get in touch with either of them after reading that newspaper clipping? What if Gale hadn't gone to bat for Noyce after the luau pig incident (The Stolen Luau Pig), and the boy had been expelled? "If Bob hadn't been able to finish at Grinnell, he would have never been introduced to the transistor, and he certainly wouldn't have attended MIT."

What Wolfe is doing is more than storytelling. He is pointing at the structure of great lives — that they are, almost always, chains of small, seemingly unconnected events, each of which had to happen for the next one to become possible. The fortuitous chain is not luck in the lottery sense. It is the accumulation of dozens of contingencies, each tiny on its own, whose collective product is an improbable outcome.

Why It's Good: The concept is constrained by the specific story we can trace backwards. Each link is identifiable. Each counterfactual is plausible. We can show, with historical precision, how removing any one link dissolves the downstream outcome.

Hard-to-Vary Details: The concept requires (1) multiple genuinely independent contingencies, (2) each of which is individually low-probability, and (3) each of which is a necessary condition for the next. Remove any condition and you have either inevitability (if all links are redundant) or randomness (if no links are necessary). The fortuitous chain sits in between.

Falsifiability: If biographies of great people showed clean, linear, planned paths — no stolen pigs, no chance newspaper articles — the concept would fail. They don't. They show chains.

Reach: This is the ex-post companion of Making Yourself a Big Target for Luck. Looking forward, you cannot engineer the chain. You can only maximize your exposure to the next link. Looking backward, the chain is clearly visible — and it is humbling. The sheer contingency of great outcomes is the best antidote to both arrogance and despair.

2024-07-12 Num 356 How The Sun Rose On Silicon Valley — Bob Noyce (Founder of Intel)

Charlie Munger

Autocatalysis — Staying in the Game Long Enough to Get Lucky

Autocatalysis is a chemistry term for a reaction where a product itself acts as a catalyst for the reaction — it self-accelerates. Munger borrows it to describe the class of business events where an external invention, discovered by someone else entirely, dramatically amplifies the value of assets you already hold. The lesson is deceptively simple: stay in the game long enough and the world will carry you forward.

Why It's Good: The explanation connects observable business outcomes to a precise causal mechanism: durable asset + external amplifier = disproportionate gain. It is constrained by the specific condition that the underlying asset must have durable intrinsic value; without that, there is nothing for the autocatalytic reaction to amplify. This rules out the easy-to-vary version ("just stay in anything and something good will happen").

Hard-to-Vary Details: The mechanism requires (1) an asset that retains value over time (Disney's film library, Coca-Cola's brand, railroad infrastructure), (2) an external technology or network effect not created by the asset holder, and (3) that asset holder remaining in the game to harvest the reaction. Remove the durability of the asset and you have luck, not autocatalysis.

Falsifiability: If durable assets routinely failed to benefit from external technological amplifiers — if the autocatalytic pattern were random rather than structural — the model would be falsified. Disney's multiple amplification events (VHS, DVD, streaming) Disney Autocatalysis — From Film Vault to Streaming Gold and the railroad-plus-shipping-container example provide the empirical test.

Reach: Applies wherever long-duration assets exist and external technological change is plausible — real estate in cities with infrastructure investment, pharmaceutical patents with expanding disease recognition, media libraries in an era of new distribution platforms.

Related: Surfing — Ride the Wave and Stay There | Advantages of Scale and the Lollapalooza Effect

2023-12-05 Num 329 Charlie Munger (the NEW Poor Charlie's Almanack)

Christian Dior

Make Yourself a Target for Luck

Paul Graham, reading biographies, noticed the same thing Senra notices after three hundred of them: "It is remarkable how much luck is involved. They discover what to work on as a result of a chance meeting, or by reading a book they happen to pick up." Graham's prescription, and Dior's life-in-evidence: you need to make yourself a big target for luck, and the way to do that is to be curious.

Dior didn't stumble into Marcel Busac by accident. He was already embedded in the Paris fashion world through a decade of work at Lelong's atelier, building the relationships that would make the introduction possible. The meeting looked like luck. The groundwork did not.

The concept clarifies something important about how people explain their own success — they tend to attribute it to chance because the causal chain is too long and diffuse to trace. But the people who get "lucky" consistently are almost always the ones who have spent years in proximity to the relevant domain, accumulating the knowledge and contacts that make serendipity possible.

Why It's Good: The explanation identifies a clear mechanism — exposure density increases the probability of a productive chance encounter, which is not the same as pure luck. It explains why curiosity predicts success without reducing success to mere persistence.

Hard-to-Vary Details: The mechanism requires (1) extended proximity to the relevant domain, not brief exposure; (2) genuine curiosity, which keeps you engaged long enough to accumulate the network; and (3) receptivity when the chance arrives, which means having done enough preparation to recognize the opportunity.

Falsifiability: If high performers consistently succeeded through deliberate strategy rather than chance meetings or random encounters, this claim would weaken. But the biographical record, across fields, consistently shows an element of serendipitous encounter — whose value was only accessible to someone already prepared.

Reach: Pasteur's "chance favors the prepared mind" is the same claim in a different coat. Warren Buffett meeting Charlie Munger at a dinner party. Jobs hearing about Xerox PARC. Fleming noticing the mold on the petri dish. The pattern repeats.

2023-12-18 Num 331 The Autobiography of Christian Dior

Oprah Winfrey

Luck is Preparation Meeting Opportunity

Oprah repeated this line so many times — across the 1991 interview, across the Stanford talk, across her public career — that Senra flagged the repetition as a signal. When someone says the same thing over and over again, it means it is really important to them. "Luck is preparation meeting opportunity." That is the formulation she kept returning to.

The phrase is a refutation of the passive luck story. In that passive version, luck happens to you — you are in the right place at the right time by accident. In Oprah's version, luck has two necessary ingredients, and you control one of them. Preparation is yours. Opportunity is not. But if the preparation is absent when opportunity arrives, you are not unlucky. You are simply unprepared.

Predictive Value: This rule of thumb correctly predicts that sustained, deliberate preparation — reading broadly, acquiring varied skills, showing up consistently — increases the rate at which people "get lucky." Oprah's own chain of discovery confirms it: she was discovered on radio by television scouts because she had spent years getting good at radio. Quincy Jones found her on TV because she had been getting good at TV. Neither discovery was planned. Both were enabled.

Explanatory Gap: The rule does not explain the precise mechanism by which preparation makes someone recognizable to the right person at the right moment. It does not account for the cases where maximum preparation meets zero opportunity for structural reasons — circumstance of birth, access to networks, historical timing. It is predictively reliable but incomplete as an explanation of why luck distributes as unevenly as it does.

2024-01-16 Num 334 Oprah In Her Own Words

Richard Garriott

Luck = Preparation × Opportunity

Garriott is unusually honest about his luck. His father was a NASA astronaut, which gave him access to Lockheed Martin's computers as a teenager. He was born at the dawn of personal computers. He had a brother with an MBA to manage the business side. These are not small advantages. But his framing is not "therefore luck explains my success" — it's "the key is that you must be paying attention to see opportunities parade past, be willing to take risks, and believe in what you're doing so much that you do it anyway."

The Latin version of this principle is older than computers: fortuna favors the prepared. What Garriott adds is the mechanism: most people see the same opportunities, but most people are not prepared to recognize them as opportunities. Preparation is a filter, not just a multiplier.

Predictive Value: This rule predicts why extraordinary circumstances alone never produce extraordinary outcomes. The same NASA connection that put a young Garriott in a computer room put other teenagers there too — but only Garriott had already spent months obsessing over D&D and text adventure games, so he knew what he was seeing. Preparation made the opportunity legible.

Explanatory Gap: This doesn't fully explain why some highly prepared people still miss their opportunities — which suggests that preparation is necessary but not sufficient, and that some random element remains irreducible. The rule is a strong heuristic, not a guarantee.

Why It's Unintuitive: People treat luck as either pure accident or pure myth. The rule says it's neither — it's the intersection of an internal state (preparation) and an external event (opportunity). You can't control opportunities, but you can dramatically improve your hit rate by building the preparation that makes them recognizable.

2022-07-15 Num 257 Richard Garriott (Video Games and Space Exploration)

TR and JP Morgan

Right Place Right Time Right Skills

A recurring pattern in the Founder's Podcast: the historically consequential figures were not simply talented — they were talented at the moment when their particular talent was maximally needed, in an environment that had been structurally prepared to reward it. Morgan, born to a banking dynasty at the dawn of industrial capitalism, is the clearest case Senra identifies.

Predictive Value

The framework predicts that even extraordinary capability will fail to produce outsized outcomes if the timing and environment are misaligned. Conversely, modest capability deployed in the right structural moment can produce enormous leverage. Morgan was not the smartest banker of his era — but he was the best-suited banker for the era of railroad consolidation.

Explanatory Gap

The rule of thumb doesn't explain why some people recognize their moment and others miss it. That requires a deeper explanation about self-knowledge, risk tolerance, and the ability to read structural change — which is a separate concept.

Senra describes this as a "recurring theme" across biographies he has read: "there's a certain few historical figures that were in the right place at the right time with the right set of skills." Morgan is his exemplar here — "proved instinctively suited to the times in which he lived" in an era of "raucous, unfettered competition, chaotic capitalism that he would try to order."

See also: Hill's Great Northern — The Railroad That Did Not Need Morgan

2020-08-30 Num 142 Teddy Roosevelt and J.P. Morgan

John D. Rockefeller

Designing Luck

Luck, in the Rockefeller framework, is not something that happens to you. It is something you engineer. The formula is simple to state and hard to live: identify your goal, catalog your resources, build a plan, and then survive long enough for circumstances to align. What ordinary people call luck is, from the inside, the payoff of sustained design.

The phrase that anchors the concept comes from Cyrus McCormick: "Luck is the remnant of design." Rockefeller adopted it wholesale, extended it, and made it personal: "I do not live by God-given luck, but I do so by planning luck." Napoleon said the same thing in different clothes — "All great events hang on a single thread. The clever man takes advantage of everything; neglects nothing that may give him some added opportunity."

Why It's Good: The explanation is mechanistic. It specifies what to do (set a goal, map resources, design a plan, survive), when to do it (before luck arrives, so you can recognize and capitalize on it), and what outcome to expect (compound advantage over time). It is falsifiable: a person who does all of these and consistently fails to convert opportunity into outcome would challenge the model. It matches the observable pattern in Rockefeller's own career — the Cleveland Massacre, the destruction of the Pennsylvania Railroad, the Clark auction — every "lucky" break was preceded by years of deliberate positioning.

Hard-to-Vary Details: The explanation requires (1) a clearly defined goal, (2) an honest inventory of existing resources, (3) a structured plan connecting the two, and (4) survivability — staying in the game long enough for luck to arrive. Removing any of these does not produce a weaker version of the same idea; it produces a different idea entirely — blind optimism, or wishful thinking.

Falsifiability: If people who plan carefully and stay solvent consistently fail to outperform people who do not plan, the explanation fails.

Reach: Designing luck applies wherever humans face large, uncertain outcomes: entrepreneurship, investing, athletics, scientific research. Charlie Munger's "fundamental algorithm of life — repeat what works" is the maintenance version of the same concept. Action Solves Everything is its operational corollary — the plan means nothing without the first move.

2023-10-21 Num 324 John D Rockefeller — 38 Letters Rockefeller Wrote to His Son
